risks: Procedural character art quality: Hand-crafting 32+ pixel arrays (4 animation states × 2+ directions × 2-4 frames each) as TypeScript number arrays is extremely error-prone. Each 16x24 array = 384 pixels that must form recognizable human shapes. Without visual feedback iteration, bad arrays = ugly characters that fail the quality bar.

Renderer restructuring for z-sort: Moving from flat grid-order tile rendering to painter's algorithm requires merging walls, furniture, and characters into a single sorted draw list. Wrong z-sort logic = visual artifacts (characters clipping through furniture, furniture rendering over walls). The current render() function structure must be significantly refactored.

Environment rendering scope: Upgrading ALL 10 tile types to 20-40 lines of multi-layer procedural art code is 200-400 new lines in renderer.ts, each requiring visual verification. Floors need HSL colorization tied to room data. Walls need depth shading. Risk of the visual feedback loop making this 3x slower than estimated.
over_engineering_flags: Wall auto-tiling with 4-bit bitmask (16 variants) from pixel-agents is complex — simpler depth shading with accent colors achieves 80% of the visual lift for 20% of the effort
RoomGrid data model could creep toward a room editor system — must stay render-only (static data, no CRUD)
challenges.recommendation: Proceed as-is — CEO mandated ALL furniture gets full treatment and quality bar is explicit. Combine floors/walls/furniture into one initiative since they all modify renderer.ts (learned pattern: avoid merge conflicts). Keep wall auto-tiling simple (depth shading, not full bitmask).

initiatives.title: Z-Sort Painter's Algorithm + Room Zone Data Model
initiatives.user_scenario: CEO sees an office with distinct colored room zones and characters that correctly overlap with furniture based on their Y position.
initiatives.scope: Add RoomGrid data model to types.ts with typed room zones (engineering, executive, conference, lobby) including department names, color themes (HSL base), and tile boundaries. Update office-layout.ts to export ROOM_GRID data mapping tile coordinates to rooms. Refactor render() in renderer.ts to use painter's algorithm — collect all renderable entities (floor/wall tiles, furniture, characters) with zY values, sort by Y, draw back-to-front. Ensure floor tiles always draw first per row, walls and furniture sort correctly, and characters overlap furniture when in front.
definition_of_done: RoomGrid type exists in types.ts with room id, display name, color theme (HSL), and tile boundary rect
office-layout.ts exports ROOM_GRID with at least 4 distinct room zones covering the full 20x14 grid
Renderer sorts all drawable entities by Y coordinate using painter's algorithm before drawing
Existing tile renderers produce no visual regressions — all 10 tile types still render correctly
TypeScript compiles and Vite builds with zero errors

initiatives.title: Floors, Walls, and Furniture Visual Overhaul
initiatives.user_scenario: CEO sees rich textured floors, detailed walls with depth shading, and furniture with wood grain, glowing monitors, and shadow detail that exceeds pixel-agents and claw-empire quality.
initiatives.scope: Upgrade all tile renderers in renderer.ts to exceed reference repo quality. Floors: replace flat fills with textured patterns (checkerboard with fake 3D lighting, wood grain) using HSL colorization from the room's color theme. Walls: add depth shading (top edge lighter, bottom darker), baseboard with room accent colors, and subtle mortar texture improvements. Furniture: upgrade all 10 tile types (desk, ceo-desk, conference, whiteboard, mailbox, bell, server-room, door, floor, wall) to 20-40 lines each with multi-layer procedural Canvas 2D — wood grain textures, monitor screens showing colored code lines, coffee mug details, keyboard keycaps, book spines on shelves, cable detail, steam effects via offset pixels.
definition_of_done: Floors use textured patterns (checkerboard or wood grain) with HSL colorization per room type — zero flat solid fills remain
Walls have visible depth shading (light top edge, dark bottom) and room-specific accent colors
All 10 tile type renderers use 20-40 lines of procedural drawing with highlights, shadows, and at least 2 detail layers
Desk monitors show colored rectangles suggesting code content, desks show wood grain texture via alternating pixel rows
TypeScript compiles and Vite builds with zero errors

initiatives.title: Multi-Frame Directional Character Sprite System
initiatives.user_scenario: CEO sees distinct agent characters at their desks with recognizable hair styles, clothing details, and poses — each agent is clearly identifiable at a glance.
initiatives.scope: Expand sprites.ts with hand-crafted 16x24 pixel array templates for multiple animation states and directions. Required states: walk (4 frames), sit (1 frame), type (2 frames), idle (2 frames). Required directions: front-facing and side-facing (left drawn, right as horizontal flip). Add distinct per-agent visual differentiation beyond palette colors — at minimum 2-3 different hair style templates and clothing variations encoded in the pixel arrays. Maintain and expand the palette system (H/K/S/P pattern from pixel-agents) for brand color tinting. Export a getSpriteFrame(name, action, direction, frameIndex) function. Keep sprite cache with offscreen canvases.
definition_of_done: Sprite templates exist for at least 4 animation states: walk (4 frames), sit (1), type (2), idle (2) — minimum 9 unique pixel arrays
Front-facing and side-facing directions supported, with side-right as horizontal flip of side-left
Each of the 5 named agents has visually distinct appearance via palette + at least 2 hair style variants across agents
getSpriteFrame(name, action, direction, frameIndex) exported and returns correct cached offscreen canvas
TypeScript compiles and Vite builds with zero errors

initiatives.title: Character Animation State Machine and Frame Cycling
initiatives.user_scenario: CEO sees agents typing at their desks with subtle 2-frame animation, walking with fluid 4-frame cycles, and idling with gentle movement — the office feels alive.
initiatives.scope: Add animation state types to types.ts tracking per-agent current action (walk/sit/type/idle), direction, and frame index. Implement animation tick logic in the rAF loop — frame cycling at appropriate rates (8fps for walk, 4fps for type/idle). Map AgentStatus to animation action (working→typing, idle→idle, waiting→idle, error→idle). Update renderer to call getSpriteFrame with current animation state instead of the static getAgentSprite. Add animation time accumulator to CanvasOffice.tsx so frame advances are independent of display refresh rate.
definition_of_done: AnimationState type in types.ts tracks action, direction, and frameIndex per agent name
Frame cycling runs at 8fps for walk and 4fps for type/idle, decoupled from display refresh rate
AgentStatus automatically maps to animation action — working agents type, idle agents idle
Renderer uses animation state to select correct sprite frame via getSpriteFrame
TypeScript compiles and Vite builds with zero errors
